title: Derenbergia densipuncta
intro: Derenbergia densipuncta, also known as Jeringa or Indian Snakeroot, is a flowering plant native to the tropical regions of Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine, offering various health benefits, including the treatment of fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, as well as its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Bioactive constituents of Derenbergia densipuncta include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Derenbergia densipuncta is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used in the form of a tincture or powder to treat various health conditions.
taxonomy: Derenbergia densipuncta is a flowering plant in the family Fabaceae. It is a member of the genus Derenbekia. However, this information is not available for Derenbergia densipuncta.

side_effects: Derenbergia densipuncta, like many plants in the Amaryllidaceae family, contains toxic compounds that can cause adverse effects when ingested or handled improperly. These compounds, including alkaloids and glycosides, can be harmful if they come into contact with the skin or are ingested in large quantities. Ingestion of Derenbergia densipuncta can lead to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Prolonged exposure to these toxic compounds can cause more severe health issues, including neurological problems and cardiac arrhythmias. Handling Derenbergia densipuncta requires proper precautions, including wearing protective gloves and eyewear.
